mcqs.question: Why is MPI_Barrier used before starting the timer in MPI timing code?

options.text: To ensure all processes are synchronized before timing begins
To initialize the MPI communication environment
To measure the communication time between processes
To allocate memory for parallel execution

mcqs.explanation: MPI_Barrier ensures all processes reach the same point in execution before the master process starts timing, providing accurate measurement of parallel execution time.

mcqs.question: Which MPI function is used to get the current time with high precision?

options.text: MPI_Wtime()
MPI_Time()
MPI_Clock()
MPI_Timestamp()

mcqs.explanation: MPI_Wtime() returns an elapsed time on the calling processor with high precision, making it ideal for performance measurements.

mcqs.question: Why is timing typically done only on the master process (rank 0)?

options.text: To avoid timing synchronization issues between processes
Only rank 0 has access to the system clock
MPI requires timing to be done on a single process
It reduces communication overhead during measurement

mcqs.explanation: Using only one process for timing avoids the complexity of synchronizing clocks across multiple processes and provides a consistent measurement reference.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of the second MPI_Barrier call after the parallel work?

options.text: To ensure all processes complete before ending the timer
To prepare for program termination
To measure barrier synchronization time
To flush MPI communication buffers

mcqs.explanation: The second barrier ensures all processes have completed their work before the master process stops timing, capturing the complete parallel execution time.

mcqs.question: What does the difference between end_time and start_time represent?

options.text: The wall-clock time for parallel execution
The CPU time used by all processes combined
The communication time only
The computation time only

mcqs.explanation: The difference represents wall-clock time (elapsed real time) for the parallel execution, including both computation and communication overhead.

mcqs.question: Why is wall-clock time preferred over CPU time for measuring MIMD program performance?

options.text: CPU time misses idle and waiting time spent during communication
Wall-clock time is always more accurate than CPU time
CPU time cannot be measured on multi-processor systems
Wall-clock time automatically includes overhead correction

mcqs.explanation: CPU time only measures active computation and misses time spent waiting for messages, synchronizing at barriers, or sitting idle due to load imbalance. Wall-clock time captures the full parallel execution experience.

mcqs.question: What is the best practice for obtaining reliable timing results from MPI programs?

options.text: Run multiple trials and report the average or minimum
Run the program once with maximum processors
Use the system clock instead of MPI_Wtime
Time only the initialization phase

mcqs.explanation: Multiple trials help account for system variations such as network congestion, OS scheduling, and cache effects. Reporting the average or minimum provides more statistically reliable results.

mcqs.question: Which MPI function can be used to aggregate timing data from all processes to find the maximum execution time?

options.text: MPI_Reduce with MPI_MAX
MPI_Gather with sorting
MPI_Bcast with timing flag
MPI_Barrier with time parameter

mcqs.explanation: MPI_Reduce with MPI_MAX operation collects the maximum time across all processes, giving the true parallel execution time since the slowest process determines overall completion.
